course_description:
Students in this course respond creatively to contemporary and future urban design and development scenarios, undertaking research and design in relation to responsive environments. The course maintains a strong focus on environmental and social sustainability and the transformative contribution of technology to emerging urban neighbourhoods. Students will interrogate multiple dimensions of the 'Smart City', including its spatial, economic, and community aspects. The role of urban design to promote and facilitate entrepreneurial activity, and contribute to the regeneration and transformation of public places is explored.

LEGACY by DESIGN. Strategies for Resilient Urbanism beyond 2032.

The Brisbane 2032 Olympic and Paralympic Games present a unique opportunity to catalyse long-term urban transformation. This studio explores how the Games can drive a meaningful Olympic Legacy—one that delivers lasting social, environmental, and economic benefits for Brisbane and beyond. Central to this vision is the creation of a connected network of high-quality public spaces (gold places) and blue-green infrastructure (green pathways) that supports climate resilience, mobility, and community wellbeing. Students will work across three scales:

City – In a 3-day workshop, teams map Brisbane’s Olympic Legacy Precincts and potential Links, developing a Blue-Green Network Map (BGNM).

Precinct – In teams, students create an Olympic Legacy Precinct Plan (OLPP) that outlines spatial strategies and urban strategies for a selected corridor.

Place – Individually, students develop a Place Design Toolkit (PDT) to implement and test detailed urban initiatives and interventions within their precinct.

Students will be also required to develop a ‘Program of Community Engagement’ (PCE) to be mapped throughout the project journey. This will encourage inclusive and participatory design that reflects local aspirations and fosters long-term community stewardship. This component of the studio aims to deepen students’ understanding of the growing emphasis on stakeholder engagement in urban regeneration, where inclusive processes are increasingly recognised as key to achieving successful, equitable, resilient, and locally responsive outcomes.

The studio advances contemporary ideas promoted in the powerful advocacy platform established by AIA, AILA, PIA and DIA for the Brisbane 2032 Olympic and Paralympic Games. This initiative supports the opportunity and urgency for Brisbane 2032 to leverage the city as the venue. It anticipates the visitor experience for this significant event relying on the quality of the urban places and their connections; and that the community will experience the most long-term benefit where strategic investments are made in delivering better places for all.

incompatible: You can't enrol in this course if you've already completed the following:
restrictions: This course is restricted for enrolment to GCUrbDevDes and MUrbDevDes. MArch students and Study Abroad students must email adp@uq.edu.au to enrol.

course_contacts.name: Associate Professor Paola Leardini
course_contacts.notes: Appointments with the Studio Principle can be made by email. Appointments should be reserved for specific problems of a personal nature. General course related issues should be raised at the start or end of scheduled lecture/studio sessions.

timetable:
The timetable for this course is available on the
UQ Public Timetable
.
Additional timetable information
This course includes a 3-day intensive workshop (Thu-Sat). Attendance and participation in this 'intensive' activity is essential to familiarise with core concepts of the course. During the workshop, students understand the city as a system of systems, learn from field experts about the Olympics and work in teams to analyse the city’s structural layers to develop a Blue-Green Network Map (BGNM) that identifies opportunities for legacy-driven transformation.
course_aims: This course aims to extend students' ability to formulate and implement urban design strategies that resolve contemporary issues of responsiveness to socio-cultural and environmental contexts.

learning_outcomes.description: Critically analyse the relationship between urban design, social and environmental systems, and their impacts, through individual and/or collaborative analysis and propositions.
Deploy a reflexive and research-driven design process.
Integrate contemporary urban strategies, practice and discourse in the design of a complex project at the urban scale, with a focus on contextual, social and environmental system opportunities.

Communicate the technical, experiential, formal and spatial qualities of an urban design proposal across a range of scales and disciplinary contexts, to a best practice standard, using emerging techniques, governance and design strategies to demonstrate urban opportunity and social, economic and environmental enhancement.

assessment_details.title: A01 : Olympic Legacy Framework (OLF)
assessment_details.category: Participation/ Student contribution, Presentation, Product/ Design
assessment_details.due_date: Presentation 4/09/2025 9:00 am Digital Submission 10/09/2025 4:00 pm Submission: Via Blackboard ULTRA
assessment_details.other_conditions: Peer assessment factor. See the conditions definitions
assessment_details.task_description: Assessment Rationale: Building on the insights and strategic planning developed during the 3-Day Workshop, each team shifts focus to the precinct scale to develop an Olympic Legacy Framework (OLF), which culminates in the Olympic Legacy Precinct Plan (OLPP). This plan explores a selected section of Brisbane’s emerging Blue-Green Network, including its surrounding open spaces, public places, and community assets. The OLF is a spatial and functional design framework that articulates a team’s vision for Olympic Legacy at the precinct scale. It must integrate ‘gold places’—high-quality, inclusive public spaces—with supporting blue-green infrastructure that enhances mobility, water management, ecological performance, and community wellbeing. Working collaboratively, each team: • Defines a precinct vision that reflects the Olympic Legacy’s values and long-term aspirations. • Establishes a set of site-specific design principles and spatial strategies to guide future development. • Identifies key urban initiatives that support social, environmental, and economic resilience. • Maps the integration of green and blue systems with public space networks, mobility corridors, and community destinations. • Investigates local communities to understand their diverse needs, values and aspirations, and proposes ideas for bold legacy initiatives—ranging from projects and policies to targets, programs, and other mechanisms—that foster meaningful community participation and long-term transformation. Both OLF and OLPP must respond to the unique characteristics of the selected precinct, while also contributing to the broader city-wide Blue-Green Network Map (BGNM) initiated during the workshop. The OLPP serves as the strategic foundation for Assessment Task 3 (A03), where students individually develop a detailed Place Design Toolkit to implement and test the OLPP’s spatial strategies at a finer grain. A01 is set as a sequence, i.e. it includes tasks in two consecutive weeks: - Presentation and Review: 4/09/2025, 10 am (video and presentation slide deck to be uploaded to Blackboard Ultra by 9 am) - Booklet digital submission: 10/09/2025, 4 pm A01 emphasises collaborative visioning, contextual analysis, and strategic design thinking—laying the groundwork for place-based interventions that can deliver a meaningful and enduring Olympic Legacy. Participation, engagement and contribution to teamwork during the intensive workshop and across the first weeks of the semester (1-6) leading to A01 will be evaluated through a Group Peer Assessment process and may affect the A01 individual grade. If, for whatever reason, you find that your group is not functioning effectively, please contact your Course Coordinator for support. A01 Deliverables 1. Video documentation (max 5 min) 2. Presentation slide deck 3. Team oral presentation (20 min team presentation + 10 min feedback) 4. Olympic Legacy Framework booklet (A4 portrait, colour) – including BGNM (finalised), vision, principles, OLPP, and Program of Community Engagement (PCE). Complex/Authentic assessment using AI and/or MT to support learning: Artificial Intelligence (AI) and Machine Translation (MT) are emerging tools that may support students in completing this assessment task. Students may appropriately use AI and/or MT in completing this assessment task. Students must clearly reference any use of AI or MT in each instance. A failure to reference generative AI or MT use may constitute student misconduct under the Student Code of Conduct. Read full task description Hide full task description
assessment_details.submission_guidelines:
The School of Architecture, Design and Planning uses Blackboard and Turnitin for assessment submission. Turnitin is accessed through the course Blackboard site. Turnitin also checks for plagiarism or instances where the original work of others is not appropriately acknowledged. Uploaded files must contain readable text and not be rasterised. Students are advised to commence assignment uploads with sufficient amount of time (consider possible technical problems with computers, internet speed, etc). After successfully submitting an assignment through Turnitin, a ‘Submission Complete!’ screen will be displayed. It is the student’s responsibility to check assignment preview and confirm successful submission. If the ‘Submission Complete!’ screen isn’t displayed, the student should regard the submission as unsuccessful. Students should download a copy of the digital receipt as proof they have submitted the assignment. Students who are experiencing upload issues must advise the Course Coordinator immediately by email and should include screenshots and a copy of the assessment for submission. To meet professional accreditation, public engagement and quality assurance obligations, digital copies of all course assessment items must be submitted in addition to any hard copy submission requirements specified in individual Course / Studio Outlines. Any physical models should be photographed and a minimum of two photographs describing the complete and full model must be included in the digital submission.
Deferral or extension
You may be able to apply for an extension .
The maximum extension allowed is 14 days. Extensions are given in multiples of 24 hours.
If a student wishes to apply for an extension, they must apply online on or before the assignment due date. When possible, it is suggested that requests are submitted 2 business days prior to the submission due date for the assignment to allow processing time. Students with outstanding applications for extensions are advised to submit their assessment by the original due date, irrespective of whether the work is complete, so that what has been done can be graded.
Group requests must fill out the group member acknowledgement form: https://my.uq.edu.au/files/35533/extension-to-group-assessment.pdf with at least 50% in agreeance.
If students are submitting formative submissions to a repository - the most recent previous submission can be marked in lieu of a late submission.
Critique panels cannot be reconvened for the presentation component of assessment when an extension has been approved. Students with granted extensions (or pending extension applications) are able to attend but are not permitted to participate in the design review.
Late submission
The late penalty for this assessment item will be calculated as follows:
First 1-hour block - initial 1 hour grace period no penalty.
Second 1-hour block - An penalty of 10% of the maximum possible mark allocated for the assessment item will be deducted
Third 1-hour block - An additional penalty of 10% of the maximum possible mark allocated for the assessment item will be deducted. (adding up to a total penalty of 20% of the maximum possible mark)
Any submissions received after three hours will not receive any marks unless an extension has been approved. Each one-hour block is recorded from the time the submission is due.
Oral presentation (Group): 100% Late Penalty. No grace period.
Group Work: If one student has an extension then this student will receive the same mark as their other group members (with agreement from at least 50% of the members of the group, and recognition of potential impacts on the other group members). If 50% of group members do not agree, the extension will not be approved.

assessment_details.title: A02: Place Design Toolkit (PDT)-DRAFT
assessment_details.due_date: Slide deck digital submission 16/10/2025 9:00 am Presentation & Review 16/10/2025 10:00 am
assessment_details.other_conditions: Peer assessed. See the conditions definitions
assessment_details.task_description: Assessment Rationale: In the second phase of the urban design studio, students work individually to develop a Place Design Toolkit (PDT) that supports the implementation of place-based urban interventions proposed in their team’s Olympic Legacy Precinct Plan (OLPP) from Assessment Task 1. The aim is to deepen the understanding of the spatial qualities and character of place envisioned in the OLPP, and to translate these into a set of actionable urban initiatives. These initiatives will serve as design interventions aligned with the OLPP’s vision, principles, and development strategies. Each student selects a specific area within their team’s precinct plan to focus on, ensuring that the team collectively produces a comprehensive and complementary suite of guidelines. Within their chosen area, individually students must: • Advance the spatial strategies established in A01. • Provide a detailed analysis of place-specific qualities and character. • Test and refine design ideas for a high-quality network of public spaces and blue-green infrastructure. • Design and test place-based engagement strategies tailored to specific local contexts. The Place Design Toolkit should propose interventions that promote social and environmental wellbeing, with a focus on: • Expanding green and open spaces. • Enhancing accessibility and comfort for outdoor leisure. • Supporting active travel networks. • Integrating nature-based water management solutions. • Mitigating urban heat island effects. This assignment challenges students to bridge strategic planning with detailed design thinking, enabling the delivery of a resilient and inclusive Olympic Legacy at the local scale. A02 requires students to develop a draft of their final A03 submission for formative feedback. Minimum submission requirements are included in the A02 Outline (Blackboard Ultra). A02 Deliverables Oral presentation (10 min presentation + 5 min feedback) Presentation slide deck (to be uploaded to Blackboard Ultra by 9 am) Complex/Authentic assessment using AI and/or MT to support learning: Artificial Intelligence (AI) and Machine Translation (MT) are emerging tools that may support students in completing this assessment task. Students may appropriately use AI and/or MT in completing this assessment task. Students must clearly reference any use of AI or MT in each instance. A failure to reference generative AI or MT use may constitute student misconduct under the Student Code of Conduct. Read full task description Hide full task description

learning_activities.learning_period: Multiple weeks From Week 1 To Week 13 (21 Jul - 26 Oct)
learning_activities.topic: Lecture Week 1 (24-26/7) - Intensive Design Studio - City Scale Day 1 - Understanding urban systems (Paola & Cathryn) AM: Project introduction + teamwork; PM: City thinking - Shaping Brisbane Day 2 - Big Ideas for 2032 Brisbane (guest lectures) AM: Green Pathways Gold Places + Visioning for the Brisbane 2032 Olympic Paralympic Games Day 3 - Understanding Olympic Legacy - the challenges and opportunities AM: What is Olympic Legacy? (Paola & Cathryn) Week 3 (7/8) - Olympic Legacy Framework: vision, principles and actors AM: Future South Bank Master Plan (guest lecture) Week 4 (14/8) - Olympic Legacy: Environmental resilience (mapping blue-green challenges and opportunities) AM: Water Sensitive Urban Design - Theory and case studies (guest lecture) Week 6 (28/08) - Olympic Legacy Precinct Plan AM: Olympic site design for shaping urban change (Paola & Cathryn) Week 8 (11/9) – Place Design Toolkit-Understanding community and places AM: Overview of A02 and A03 (Paola & Cathryn) Week 9 (18/9) – Place Design Toolkit AM: What is a Place Design Toolkit? Exploring the elements of effective design guidelines (Paola & Cathryn) Week 13 (23/10) - Place Design Toolkit: finalisation and presentation plan AM: Communicate with impact (guest lecture) Learning outcomes: L01, L03, L04

learning_activities.learning_period: Multiple weeks From Week 1 To Week 14 (21 Jul - 02 Nov)
learning_activities.topic: Design Studio Week 1 (24-26/7) - Intensive Design Studio - City Scale (teamwork) Day 1 - Team building; Urban analysis (SWOT) + Roundtable (challenges and opportunity for change at city scale) Day 2 - Blue-green Network Map – contextual research and mapping Day 3 - Learning from the Olympics - case study analysis Week 2 (31/7) - Week 2 - Blue-green Network Map and ideas (teamwork) - Sharing the Blue-green Network Map - Case study presentation: Olympic Legacy strategies (identified strategies for Brisbane 2032) - Team building and formation + Precinct selection - Precinct structural layers: theory, research, mapping Week 3 (7/8) - Olympic Legacy Framework: Guiding change - Workshop: Developing visions and urban design strategies workshop (teamwork) Week 4 (14/8) - Olympic Legacy: Environmental resilience (mapping blue-green challenges and opportunities) - Workshop: Precinct's blue-green infrastructure for environmental resilience (teamwork) Week 6 (28/8) - Olympic Legacy Precinct Plan: Spatial strategies - Workshop: Spatial strategies towards OLPP Week 7 (4/9) - Olympic Legacy Framework (A01): Presentation Week 8 (11/9) - Mapping ‘golden places’ within the OLPP (teamwork) - Team discussion, mapping, presentation to other teams, peer feedback and individual selection of an urban initiative Week 9 (18/9) - What is the “Place Design Toolkit”? - Understanding urban design guidelines (examples). Searching, sharing, discussing Week 10 (25/9) - Developing the Place Design Toolkit: Testing ideas - Team and individual tutoring section and peer feedback Week 11 (9/10) - Place Design Toolkit: Draft - Team and individual tutoring section and peer feedback Week 12 (16/10) - Place Design Toolkit (A02): Presentation for formative feedback - Industry and peer feedback Week 13 (23/10) - Place Design Toolkit: Communication plan - Team and individual tutoring section and peer feedback Week 14 (30/10) - Place Design Toolkit (A03): Final presentation Learning outcomes: L01, L02, L03

Excursion Week 5 - Legacy – Social resilience (mapping communities and their needs) - Visit to Ipswich Central - Council and community led regeneration project - Workshop: Strategies for community resilience (precinct scale) Learning outcomes: L03

policies_and_procedures: University policies and procedures apply to all aspects of student life.
